revision.text:
<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:伊藤富雄]] {{Infobox person|birth_date=1891|death_date={{death date|1968|1|29}}|birth_place={{ill|Nakasu|qid=Q17174642|1=Nakasu}}|gender={{ill|male|qid=Q6581097|1=Male (gender)}}|nationality={{ill|Empire of Japan|qid=Q188712|1=Empire of Japan}}; {{ill|Japan|qid=Q17|1=Japan}}|occupation={{ill|historian|qid=Q201788|1=Historian}}; {{ill|politician|qid=Q82955|1=Politician}}|title={{ill|vice-governor of Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q104604740|1=vice-governor of Nagano Prefecture}}}}
{{Refimprove|date=2019-01}} '''Tomio Itō''' (伊藤 富雄, October 23, 1891 – January 29, 1968) was a Japanese {{ill|Politician|en|Politician|qid=Q82955|lt=Politician|1=Politician}} and [[:en:local historian|local historian]].
== Biography == Tomio Itō was born in [[Nakasu Village]], [[Suwa District, Nagano Prefecture|Suwa District]], {{ill|Nagano Prefecture|en|Nagano Prefecture|qid=Q127877|lt=Nagano Prefecture|1=Nagano Prefecture}} (now part of {{ill|Suwa, Nagano|en|Suwa, Nagano|qid=Q846338|lt=Suwa, Nagano|1=Suwa, Nagano}}). He attended [[Nagano Prefectural Suwa High School]] (formerly Suwa Middle School) but dropped out.
He worked in agriculture and became the chairman of the Nakasu Village Industrial Cooperative (now [[JA Shinshu Suwa]]) in {{ill|1941|en|1941|qid=Q5231|lt=1941|1=1941}}. In {{ill|1945|en|1945|qid=Q5240|lt=1945|1=1945}}, he also served as mayor of Nakasu Village.
In {{ill|1947|en|1947|qid=Q5263|lt=1947|1=1947}}, he became the chairman of the Nagano Prefectural branch of the {{ill|Japan Socialist Party|en|Japan Socialist Party|qid=Q1144146|lt=Japan Socialist Party|1=Japan Socialist Party}} and served as vice governor under Governor [[Torao Hayashi]]. However, in {{ill|1948|en|1948|qid=Q5165|lt=1948|1=1948}}, he resigned as vice governor after a confrontation with the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|U.S. Occupation Forces]]' civil affairs officer for Nagano Prefecture.
In {{ill|1949|en|1949|qid=Q5188|lt=1949|1=1949}}, Itō ran for election to the {{ill|House of Representatives (Japan)|en|House of Representatives (Japan)|qid=Q659587|lt=House of Representatives|1=House of Representatives (Japan)}} in the [[24th Japanese general election]] as a member of the {{ill|Japanese Communist Party|en|Japanese Communist Party|qid=Q641600|lt=Japanese Communist Party|1=Japanese Communist Party}}, but he was not elected.<ref>Torao Hayashi, ''Kono Michi Junen'' (A Decade of This Path), p. 46.</ref> Subsequently, he devoted himself to researching the history of [[Suwa Shrine]] and other aspects of local history.
== Related Books == * [[Rintarō Itō]] (ed.), ''Collected Works of Tomio Itō'', Nagai Publishing, 1987. * Torao Hayashi, ''Kono Michi Junen'', {{ill|Sankei Shimbun|en|Sankei Shimbun|qid=Q1197261|lt=Sankei Shimbun|1=Sankei Shimbun}}, 1959.
== See Also == * [[Naoichi Miyachi]]
